title: Comparing the precision of two digital PCR applications for copy number comparisons in protists
author: Gross, Megan
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Microorganisms play key roles in ecosystem functioning, making reliable methods for assessing their abundance and dynamics essential. Advances in molecular technologies now enable their quantification in environmental samples based on DNA marker genes, providing alternatives to traditional microscopic counting. Among these, digital PCR has emerged as a powerful tool for detecting and quantifying organisms based on their gene copy numbers, with numerous platforms that are currently available. However, these platforms differ in their underlying technologies, and comparative studies that evaluate the performance and reproducibility remain limited. Here we compared different platform parameters including sensitivity, accuracy, precision and reproducibility across the QX200 digital droplet PCR from Bio-Rad and the QIAcuity One nanoplate-based digital PCR from QIAGEN. We used synthetic oligonucleotides in addition to DNA extracted from varying cell numbers of the ciliate  Paramecium tetraurelia  and additionally tested the impact of two restriction enzymes on gene copy number quantification. Both platforms demonstrated similar detection and quantification limits and yielded high precision across most analyses. We found a general tendency of more precise quantification using the HaeIII restriction enzyme instead of EcoRI, especially for the QX200 system. Although accuracy was affected for both platforms using synthetic oligonucleotides, gene copy number estimates from ciliate DNA were reproducible between platforms and showed a linear trend for an increasing number of cells for both platforms. These findings highlight the importance of cross-platform evaluations to ensure robust and reproducible gene copy number analysis in unicellular eukaryotes and support a potential broader application of digital PCR in environmental monitoring studies.
